title: The Shape Invariant and Lagrangian Intersections
speaker: Richard Hind
abstract: We will outline the proof of an intersection result between embedded Lagrangian tori and certain 1 parameter families of product Lagrangian tori in the 4 dimensional symplectic cylinder. The theorem can be applied to give new computations of the shape invariant, describing the Lagrangian tori in some Toric domains, and therefore to produce Symplectic embedding obstructions. This is joint work with Ely Kerman.

proof_stages.label: Toric-domain setting and Hamiltonian shape invariant
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the four-dimensional standard symplectic setting, introduces product Lagrangian tori and toric domains, formulates the Hamiltonian toric-domain embedding question, and defines the Hamiltonian shape invariant as the set of product tori globally Hamiltonian-embeddable into a domain.

proof_stages.label: Capacity-like behavior and limitations of shape
proof_stages.summary: The shape is shown to contain the symmetrized moment image, scale correctly, and be monotone under Hamiltonian embeddings, while known computations and strip phenomena reveal both its usefulness and the information it loses.

proof_stages.label: Main Lagrangian intersection theorem and sharpness of K
proof_stages.summary: The central theorem says that a global Hamiltonian image of L_{r,s} lying in the cylinder must intersect the codimension-one family K, with a stronger polydisk variant, and the hypotheses and possible replacement of K are tested against displacement and packing phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Shape corollaries from the intersection theorem
proof_stages.summary: The intersection theorem is applied to compute shapes exactly for broad classes of toric domains, giving the symmetrized moment image together with a strip of width one half and excluding any additional shape points.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction setup, compactification, and exactness strategy
proof_stages.summary: For the proof, one assumes a Hamiltonian image torus in the cylinder is disjoint from K, places it in a large polydisk, compactifies to S^2 x S^2, removes axes to identify the complement with a cotangent-type region, and reduces the desired contradiction to showing relative exactness in the correct homology class.

proof_stages.label: Neck-stretched finite-energy foliation and area control
proof_stages.summary: After stretching the neck along the two Lagrangian tori, a finite-energy foliation by spheres and broken planes is analyzed, with area and monotonicity estimates forcing the relevant broken curves to be paired area-r planes, the projection circles to be disjoint with the correct separation, and the Maslov-two section areas to match.

proof_stages.label: Homologically nontrivial case and relative exactness contradiction
proof_stages.summary: If the image torus is homologically nontrivial in the axes complement, the equal-area planes and Maslov-two sections give zero-area cylinders in the two generators, hence relative exactness, so exact Lagrangian intersection results force an intersection with the product torus.

proof_stages.label: Replacement axes from high-degree curves
proof_stages.summary: In the remaining homologically trivial case, high-degree holomorphic curves with many Lagrangian constraints are stretched, bad breaking is excluded, the resulting type-three buildings are pushed off to controlled symplectic spheres, and blow-up/blow-down surgery produces new axes for which both tori are homologically nontrivial and relatively exact, yielding the final contradiction.

examples.name: Necessity tests for the intersection-theorem hypotheses
examples.note: A horizontal Hamiltonian shift for small r and the global Hamiltonian equivalence between L_{r,s} and L_{s,r} show why the restrictions r>1/2 and s>1 cannot simply be omitted.
examples.engagement_reason: he tests the intersection theorem hypotheses by giving counter-scenarios when r, s, or the set K condition is omitted

passes.reason: The small-r, small-s, and too-small-K cases probe the theorem’s critical hypotheses and are deployed as failures when those hypotheses are removed.
the threshold cases for r and s show the intersection theorem fails if its hypotheses are weakened

the boundary regimes such as r≤1/2 or s≤1 are used to make the intersection conclusion fail when the hypotheses are dropped.

examples.name: Finite Lagrangian packing in a polydisk
examples.note: Disjoint area-s circles in a disk, crossed with the area-r circle, give a packing of Lagrangian tori that illustrates attempts to replace K by a smaller obstruction and why such replacements are delicate.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the finite packing by choosing circles in a disk, forming product tori, tracking the overlap area g, and applying intersection/displacement results
